title: नीतिशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री नीतिशतकम् ॥

॥ भर्तृहरिविरचितम् ॥

बोद्धारो मत्सरग्रस्ताः प्रभवः स्मयदूषिताः।
अबोधोपहताश्चान्ये जीर्णमङ्गे सुभाषितम्॥ १॥

विद्या नाम नरस्य रूपमधिकं प्रच्छन्नगुप्तं धनं
विद्या भोगकरी यशःसुखकरी विद्या गुरूणां गुरुः।
विद्या बन्धुजनो विदेशगमने विद्या परा देवता
विद्या राजसु पूज्यते न हि धनं विद्याविहीनः पशुः॥ २॥

साहित्यसंगीतकलाविहीनः
साक्षात्पशुः पुच्छविषाणहीनः।
तृणं न खादन्नपि जीवमानः
तद्भागधेयं परमं पशूनाम्॥ ३॥

केयूराणि न भूषयन्ति पुरुषं हारा न चन्द्रोज्ज्वला
न स्नानं न विलेपनं न कुसुमं नालंकृता मूर्धजाः।
वाण्येका समलंकरोति पुरुषं या संस्कृता धार्यते
क्षीयन्ते खलु भूषणानि सततं वाग्भूषणं भूषणम्॥ ४॥

परिवर्तिनि संसारे मृतः को वा न जायते।
स जातो येन जातेन याति वंशः समुन्नतिम्॥ ५॥

नमन्ति फलिनो वृक्षा नमन्ति गुणिनो जनाः।
शुष्ककाष्ठं च मूर्खश्च न नमन्ति कदाचन॥ ६॥

उद्यमेन हि सिध्यन्ति कार्याणि न मनोरथैः।
न हि सुप्तस्य सिंहस्य प्रविशन्ति मुखे मृगाः॥ ७॥

दुर्जनः परिहर्तव्यो विद्ययालंकृतो यदि।
मणिना भूषितः सर्पः किमसौ न भयंकरः॥ ८॥

परोक्षे कार्यहन्तारं प्रत्यक्षे प्रियवादिनम्।
वर्जयेत्तादृशं मित्रं विषकुम्भं पयोमुखम्॥ ९॥

येषां न विद्या न तपो न दानं
ज्ञानं न शीलं न गुणो न धर्मः।
ते मर्त्यलोके भुवि भारभूताः
मनुष्यरूपेण मृगाश्चरन्ति॥ १०॥

॥ इति श्री भर्तृहरिकृत नीतिशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक) सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Niti Shatakam ||

|| Bhartrihari-virachitam ||

Boddharo matsara-grastah prabhavah smaya-dushitah |
Abodha-upahatash-chanye jirnam-ange subhashitam || 1 ||

Vidya nama narasya rupam-adhikam prachchhanna-guptam dhanam
Vidya bhoga-kari yashah-sukha-kari vidya gurunam guruh |
Vidya bandhu-jano videsha-gamane vidya para devata
Vidya rajasu pujyate na hi dhanam vidya-vihinah pashuh || 2 ||

Sahitya-sangita-kala-vihinah
Sakshat-pashuh puchchha-vishana-hinah |
Trinam na khadann-api jivamanah
Tad-bhaag-dheyam paramam pashunam || 3 ||

Keyurani na bhushayanti purusham hara na chandrojjvala
Na snanam na vilepanam na kusumam na-alankrita murdhajaah |
Vany-eka samalank-aroti purusham ya sanskrita dharyate
Kshiyante khalu bhushanani satatam vag-bhushanam bhushanam || 4 ||

Parivartini samsare mritah ko va na jayate |
Sa jato yena jatena yati vamshah samunnatim || 5 ||

Namanti phalino vriksha namanti gunino janah |
Shushka-kashtham cha murkhash-cha na namanti kadachana || 6 ||

Udyamena hi sidhyanti karyani na manorathaih |
Na hi suptasya simhasya pravishanti mukhe mrigah || 7 ||

Durjanah parihartvyo vidyaya-alankrito yadi |
Manina bhushitah sarpah kim-asau na bhayamkarah || 8 ||

Parokshe karya-hantaram pratyakshe priya-vadinam |
Varjayet-tadrisham mitram visha-kumbham payo-mukham || 9 ||

Yesham na vidya na tapo na danam
Jnanam na shilam na guno na dharmah |
Te martya-loke bhuvi bhara-bhutah
Manushya-rupena mrigash-charanti || 10 ||

|| Iti Shri Bhartrihari-krita Niti Shatakam (Pramukh Shlok) Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: विद्वान मत्सरग्रस्त हैं, प्रभावशाली अहंकार से दूषित हैं, अन्य अज्ञानता से पीड़ित — इस कारण सुभाषित शरीर पर जीर्ण हो जाता है। विद्या मनुष्य का सर्वोत्तम रूप है, छुपा हुआ धन है, भोग-यश-सुख देने वाली है, गुरुओं की गुरु है, विदेश में बन्धु है, परम देवता है — राजाओं में विद्या पूजी जाती है धन नहीं, विद्याहीन पशु है। साहित्य-संगीत-कला से हीन व्यक्ति साक्षात् पुच्छ-सींग रहित पशु है। केयूर, हार, स्नान, विलेपन, कुसुम पुरुष को नहीं सजाते — केवल संस्कृत वाणी ही सच्चा भूषण है। फलदार वृक्ष झुकते हैं, गुणी लोग नम्र होते हैं — सूखी लकड़ी और मूर्ख कभी नहीं झुकते। उद्यम से कार्य सिद्ध होते हैं मनोरथों से नहीं — सोते सिंह के मुख में हिरण नहीं आते।
benefits: Inspires pursuit of knowledge as the highest treasure
Teaches practical wisdom for navigating worldly life
Encourages effort and diligence over wishful thinking
Sharpens judgment about character and relationships
description: Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, the Niti Shatakam (नीतिशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Rooted in Niti Shatakam by Bhartrihari (5th Century), this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to sharpen judgment about character and relationships and inspire pursuit of knowledge as the highest treasure. It is also said to teach practical wisdom for navigating worldly life. This sacred text is ideally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima and Vasant Panchami for enhanced blessings. The text contains 10 verses and takes around 10 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Niti Shatakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Niti Shatakam by Bhartrihari in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Key verses on ethics, wisdom and righteous conduct.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Niti Shatakam by Bhartrihari?
seoFaqs.answer: The Niti Shatakam (नीतिशतकम् — 'Hundred Verses on Niti / Ethics') is a celebrated Sanskrit didactic poem of approximately 100 shlokas composed by the great king-poet Bhartrihari (traditionally placed in the 5th century CE), the elder brother of Vikramaditya of Ujjain. It is the first of Bhartrihari's renowned Shataka-trayam (the trilogy of three centuries) — Niti Shataka (on ethics and worldly wisdom), Sringara Shataka (on love and beauty) and Vairagya Shataka (on dispassion and renunciation). The Niti Shataka is the supreme Sanskrit collection of subhashita (well-spoken aphorisms) on dharma, friendship, character, learning, the company of the wise and the wicked, the value of effort, the dignity of self-respect, and the conduct of kings — and has been memorised by Sanskrit students across India for over 1500 years. (Source: Bhartrihari Shataka-trayam, classical Sanskrit tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Bhartrihari, the composer of the Niti Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Maharaja Bhartrihari (also called Bhartrhari) was a Sanskrit poet, grammarian and philosopher traditionally identified as the elder brother of King Vikramaditya of Ujjain (c. 5th century CE). The legend records that Bhartrihari was a powerful king who, upon discovering the betrayal of a beloved queen (the famous story of the magic apple), renounced the throne and lived as a yogi in a cave near Gorakhnath in the Vindhyas, where he composed the three shatakas reflecting his journey from worldly engagement (Niti) through love (Sringara) to final renunciation (Vairagya). He is also credited with the Vakyapadiya — the supreme classical Sanskrit treatise on the philosophy of language and grammar (sphota-vada). His three shatakas are among the most quoted Sanskrit works in all of India, and his cave at Bhartrihari Gufa near Chittorgarh and Alwar is still a pilgrimage site. (Source: Bhartrihari hagiography; classical Sanskrit literary tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Vidya nama narasya rupam adhikam — the famous verse on knowledge?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 2 of the Niti Shatakam is one of the most quoted Sanskrit shlokas on the supreme value of education: Vidya nama narasya rupam-adhikam prachchhanna-guptam dhanam, vidya bhoga-kari yashah-sukha-kari vidya gurunam guruh; vidya bandhu-jano videsha-gamane vidya para devata, vidya rajasu pujyate na hi dhanam vidya-vihinah pashuh — 'Vidya (knowledge / education) is a person's superior beauty; it is a hidden, well-guarded wealth; it is the giver of enjoyment, fame and happiness; it is the guru of all gurus; it is one's relative in foreign lands; it is the supreme deity; in royal courts vidya is honoured, not wealth; one without vidya is a beast.' This single verse has been quoted in Indian school textbooks for centuries as the supreme statement of why education matters more than any other possession. (Source: Niti Shatakam verse 2.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous lion-and-deer verse on effort (udyama)?
seoFaqs.answer: One of the most beloved verses of the Niti Shatakam teaches the supreme value of personal effort over passive desire: Udyamena hi sidhyanti karyani na manorathaih, na hi suptasya simhasya pravishanti mukhe mrigah — 'Tasks are accomplished only by udyama (effort, exertion), never by manoratha (mere wishing); deer do not enter the mouth of a sleeping lion (no matter how powerful the lion may be).' This verse has become a foundational shloka of Indian work ethic, quoted in business schools, sports academies and motivational settings worldwide. Even the king of the jungle, the most powerful lion, must hunt — the lion's strength is useless without action. The verse encapsulates the karma-yoga teaching of the Bhagavad Gita in two lines and is one of the first Sanskrit shlokas every Indian child memorises. (Source: Niti Shatakam verse 7.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the structure and main themes of the Niti Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Niti Shatakam is organised into approximately ten paddhatis (sections) of ten verses each, covering the principal themes of niti (ethical, practical wisdom): (1) Murkha-paddhati — on fools and the impossibility of pleasing them, (2) Vidvat-paddhati — on the dignity and supremacy of the learned, (3) Manashaurya-paddhati — on self-respect and courage, (4) Artha-paddhati — on wealth, its uses and the hollow honour given to the wealthy, (5) Durjana-paddhati — on the wicked and the danger of their company, (6) Sujana-paddhati — on the noble and the joy of their friendship, (7) Paropakara-paddhati — on selfless service to others, (8) Dhairya-paddhati — on steadfastness in adversity, (9) Daiva-paddhati — on destiny and human effort, and (10) Karma-paddhati — on the supremacy of action and prarabdha. The Niti Shatakam page on Stotra preserves ten of the most celebrated verses representing each section. (Source: Bhartrihari Niti Shataka structure, classical Sanskrit literary tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Niti Shatakam be recited and what are its benefits?
seoFaqs.answer: The Niti Shatakam is most powerfully recited and studied on Vasant Panchami (Magha Shukla Panchami — the festival of Saraswati and the day of vidya-arambha when children begin their education), Guru Purnima (the supreme festival of the spiritual teacher), every Wednesday (Budhvar — the day of Mercury, planet of intellect) and every Thursday (Guruvar — the day of Brihaspati, the divine guru). Tradition assigns the following blessings to sincere study and contemplation of these verses: (1) cultivation of viveka (discrimination) between the noble and the wicked, (2) sharpening of the intellect through contact with the supreme Sanskrit literary mind, (3) practical wisdom for navigating the workplace, family, society and politics, (4) the cultivation of self-respect, courage and dignity in adversity, (5) protection from bad company through clear discernment of character, (6) inspiration to make personal effort the foundation of every undertaking, and (7) preparation of the mind for the higher renunciation taught in the Vairagya Shataka. (Source: Bhartrihari literary tradition; Sanskrit subhashita tradition.)
